metadata.method: Applied BL-171 6-dimension rubric (1-5 integer scale) to 6 MockInferenceBackend responses + 4 hand-crafted responses spanning the full quality spectrum

responses.label: Opening scene
responses.responseText:
You awaken on cold, damp stone. The air tastes of dust and ancient parchment. A flickering oil lamp rests in your hand, its amber glow revealing crumbling walls covered in faded glyphs. Rubble chokes the stairway above — no going back that way.

> 1. Examine the glyphs on the walls
> 2. Head north through the stone archway
> 3. Search the rubble for anything useful

rationale.D1: Strong logical flow: awaken → sense surroundings → discover constraint (rubble). Each sentence builds on the last. No contradictions.
rationale.D2: Appropriate response to 'look around' — describes full room. Doesn't address a specific sub-action since this is an opening scene, but covers the expected scope.
rationale.D3: Three distinct, scene-specific suggestions (glyphs, archway, rubble) all reference visible objects. Could be slightly more creative for a 5.
rationale.D4: Atmospheric sensory language ('tastes of dust', 'amber glow', 'crumbling walls'). Dark tone maintained. Not quite sardonic enough for a 5.
rationale.D5: Turn 1 — limited prior state to track. References oil lamp (inventory) and rubble (location feature). Scored 3 because there's no prior state to demonstrate tracking of.
rationale.D6: 53 words narrative, slightly below the 60-90 target. Complete thought, not truncated, but could be denser.

responses.label: Exploration corridor
responses.responseText:
The corridor stretches ahead, its vaulted ceiling lost in shadow. Bioluminescent fungus clings to the stones overhead, casting an eerie blue-green glow that makes your shadow dance. Water drips somewhere ahead in a steady, hypnotic rhythm.

> 1. Follow the sound of dripping water
> 2. Examine the luminous fungus closely
> 3. Continue cautiously down the corridor

rationale.D1: Cohesive scene description: visual (glow, shadow) to auditory (dripping). Cause-effect: fungus creates glow which makes shadow dance.
rationale.D2: 3 because this is a generic exploration response. If player said 'listen carefully' (S9 prompt), auditory detail is present but not foregrounded — visual dominates.
rationale.D3: Three contextual suggestions. 'Follow dripping water' and 'examine fungus' are scene-specific. 'Continue cautiously' is slightly generic but still valid.
rationale.D4: 'Eerie', 'hypnotic rhythm', 'lost in shadow' — solid atmospheric language. Misses the sardonic edge for a 5.
rationale.D5: Consistent with underground archive setting. References fungus from Main Hall location data. No contradictions, but no active state tracking.
rationale.D6: 48 words narrative — below the 60 minimum target. Good prose density but needs 12+ more words.

responses.label: Discovery (hidden alcove)
responses.responseText:
Your fingers trace the carved stone and find a hidden seam. With a grinding protest, a section of wall pivots inward, revealing a narrow alcove. Inside, a brass key gleams atop a dusty leather journal. The air here smells of cedar and old ink.

> 1. Take the brass key
> 2. Open the leather journal
> 3. Examine the alcove for traps

rationale.D1: Excellent cause-and-effect chain: trace stone → find seam → wall pivots → alcove revealed → items visible. Each sentence builds on the last naturally.
rationale.D2: Good response to an object manipulation command. The physical action (fingers tracing) maps to the command to 'reach under/examine'. Slight mismatch since it describes wall rather than desk.
rationale.D3: Three perfectly distinct, scene-specific suggestions. Each references a newly-discovered object. They offer meaningfully different paths (acquire, read, investigate).
rationale.D4: 'Grinding protest', 'cedar and old ink' — strong sensory details (sound, smell). Not purely visual. Atmospheric but not distinctively sardonic.
rationale.D5: Introduces new items consistently. Doesn't reference prior state, but as a discovery scene, this is expected.
rationale.D6: 51 words — below 60 target. Dense and well-crafted but short.

responses.label: NPC encounter (ghost archivist)
responses.responseText:
A pale figure materializes at the edge of your lamplight — translucent, flickering like a candle flame. The ghost of an archivist, spectacles perched on a nose that barely exists. It regards you with hollow eyes and speaks in a voice like rustling pages.

> 1. Ask the ghost about this place
> 2. Show the journal to the apparition
> 3. Back away slowly

rationale.D1: Strong visual description building from appearance to speech. Cohesive scene. Missing actual dialogue content for a full 5 on an NPC encounter.
rationale.D2: 3 because 'talk to the woman' would expect actual dialogue. This describes the NPC but doesn't deliver conversational content. Setup only.
rationale.D3: Good suggestions: ask, show item, retreat. 'Show the journal' is particularly clever — references a specific inventory item. All contextually distinct.
rationale.D4: Standout tone: 'nose that barely exists', 'voice like rustling pages' — sardonic, literary, memorable. This is marketing-excerpt quality prose.
rationale.D5: Archivist context is appropriate to the Archive setting. No contradictions. Doesn't reference specific prior state.
rationale.D6: 53 words — below 60 target. The description is rich but there's room for actual NPC dialogue.

responses.label: Flooded passage tension
responses.responseText:
The water rises to your ankles as you press deeper into the flooded passage. Something slithers past your leg beneath the black surface. Your lamp gutters, threatening to die. Ahead, a faint phosphorescent glow promises dry ground — if you can reach it.

> 1. Wade forward toward the glow
> 2. Use the lamp to peer into the water
> 3. Retreat to higher ground

rationale.D1: Tension builds effectively: water rising → something slithers → lamp gutters → hope ahead. Clear cause-and-effect.
rationale.D2: 3 because this is a generic progression response. Doesn't strongly address any specific player command.
rationale.D3: Standard forward/examine/retreat triad. Valid but generic — 'wade forward' and 'retreat' are directional defaults, not scene-specific discoveries.
rationale.D4: 'Something slithers', 'lamp gutters', 'black surface' — atmospheric, uses touch and visual. Good but not sardonic.
rationale.D5: Consistent with flooded passage. References lamp (inventory). No contradictions.
rationale.D6: 52 words — below 60 target. Effective but short.

responses.label: Resolution (vault opened)
responses.responseText:
The ancient lock yields to the brass key with a satisfying click. The vault door swings open on silent hinges, revealing shelves of crystalline tablets glowing with inner fire. Each one holds a story — a memory of a world long drowned. You have found the heart of the Archive.

> 1. Read the nearest crystal tablet
> 2. Search for the master codex
> 3. Look for another exit from the vault

rationale.D1: Clean cause-effect: key used → lock yields → door opens → contents revealed. Culminating discovery feels earned.
rationale.D2: Directly responds to 'use brass key on lock'. Narrates the action and its consequence. Doesn't note key consumption (per adventure data, it's consumable).
rationale.D3: Scene-specific: crystal tablet, codex, exit. Each is a different interaction type (read, search, navigate).
rationale.D4: 'World long drowned', 'crystalline tablets glowing with inner fire' — literary, atmospheric. 'Heart of the Archive' ties to the game's thematic core.
rationale.D5: References brass key (inventory item) being used. Implies state change (vault now open). Best state tracking of the 6 mocks.
rationale.D6: 55 words — below 60 target but close. Complete, dense prose.

responses.label: Fourth-wall break / chatbot mode
responses.responseText:
Sure! As your AI game master, I can help you with that. You are currently in a room with some exits. You can go north, south, or east. There are also some items around. Let me know what you'd like to do next and I'll describe what happens!

> 1. Go north
> 2. Go south
> 3. Try something else

rationale.D1: Readable sentences but no narrative coherence — just a generic description with no scene-specific detail, no atmosphere, no connection between sentences.
rationale.D2: If player said 'help', the response provides generic guidance, but ignores the in-world context entirely. Partial address of intent.
rationale.D3: Suggestions are generic placeholders ('go north', 'go south', 'try something else'). 'Try something else' is not actionable. No scene-specific references.
rationale.D4: Complete tone failure. 'Sure!' is chatbot-cheerful. 'As your AI game master' breaks the fourth wall explicitly. No atmospheric language whatsoever.
rationale.D5: No location-specific detail, no inventory reference, no acknowledgment of any game state. Generic to the point of being stateless.
rationale.D6: 52 words — acceptable range but every word is filler.

responses.label: Generic stub response
responses.responseText:
You try to eat the fungus. It tastes bad. You spit it out.

> 1. Look around
> 2. Go back
> 3. Wait

rationale.D1: Technically coherent (action → result → consequence) but so minimal it conveys nothing. No narrative texture or scene-building.
rationale.D2: Actually parses the 'eat the ceiling fungus' command correctly and provides a plausible outcome. Correct intent, minimal execution.
rationale.D3: Three suggestions present and formatted correctly, but completely generic. 'Wait' is particularly unhelpful. No scene references.
rationale.D4: Flat, clinical tone. No atmosphere, no sensory detail beyond 'tastes bad'. Not chatbot-cheerful, but not interactive fiction either.
rationale.D5: References fungus (location feature) but provides no other context. Barely acknowledges the world exists.
rationale.D6: 16 words — severely under the 40-word acceptable minimum. Stub response that would feel empty in the terminal UI.

responses.label: Atmospheric listening scene
responses.responseText:
You close your eyes and let the darkness fill your ears. The dripping resolves into three distinct sources — a slow, fat plop to your left, a tinny patter ahead, and something deeper, almost rhythmic, like breathing from below the waterline. The walls amplify each sound into a wet cathedral of echoes. Your lamp flame hisses in protest at the damp air.

> 1. Investigate the rhythmic sound beneath the water
> 2. Follow the tinny patter ahead to its source
> 3. Press your ear against the wall to listen deeper

rationale.D1: Flawless narrative flow: eyes close → darkness fills ears → sounds resolve into three sources → walls amplify → lamp reacts. Each sentence builds on the last with cause-and-effect.
rationale.D2: Perfectly addresses 'listen carefully' — foregrounds auditory detail exclusively. Three distinct sound sources demonstrate precise parsing of the 'listen' verb.
rationale.D3: Each suggestion references a specific sound source from the narrative. They offer meaningfully different investigation paths. 'Press ear against wall' is a non-obvious, curiosity-rewarding option.
rationale.D4: 'Wet cathedral of echoes', 'breathing from below the waterline', 'lamp flame hisses in protest' — sardonic personification, literary metaphor, multi-sensory. Marketing-excerpt quality.
rationale.D5: References lamp (inventory), damp air and water (Flooded Passage features), wall acoustics. Active world-state incorporation.
rationale.D6: 76 words — precisely within the 60-90 target. Every sentence earns its place. Suggestions are crisp (7-9 words each).

responses.label: Puzzle attempt with state confusion
responses.responseText:
You turn the dials on the pedestal, aligning the star symbols you remember from the mural upstairs. The mechanism groans and something clicks deep within the stone. But wait — didn't you already use the cipher wheel? The last disc won't budge. A faint hum rises from the Codex itself, its black cover seeming to pulse. The air grows warmer.

> 1. Force the last disc into position
> 2. Re-examine the cipher wheel for clues
> 3. Touch the pulsing Codex

rationale.D1: Mostly coherent but the 'didn't you already use the cipher wheel?' introduces confusion — the player hasn't used it yet, they're entering the combination. The mid-response contradiction undermines the flow.
rationale.D2: Correctly interprets 'enter the combination' as turning dials and aligning symbols. Good physical narration of the puzzle interaction.
rationale.D3: Three distinct suggestions. 'Force the disc' vs 're-examine' vs 'touch Codex' offers genuine tactical variety. All reference scene-specific objects.
rationale.D4: 'Mechanism groans', 'black cover seeming to pulse', 'air grows warmer' — atmospheric tension building. Good sensory range.
rationale.D5: Significant state confusion: questions whether cipher wheel was 'already used' when the player is actively using it. This would confuse a player reading the transcript. The disc-won't-budge complication is interesting narratively but unjustified by game state.
rationale.D6: 67 words — precisely within 60-90 target. Good density.

criticalFailures.note: 'As your AI game master' breaks fiction entirely
